title: Europe from Scratch: Visions for a New European Order
gist: An eleven-essay Carnegie Europe compilation asks experts to redesign the European order from a blank slate; the dominant answer is not a single federal leap but structured differentiation (multi-speed integration, modular foreign policy, an EU 'avant-garde' outside the treaties), paired with proposals to rebuild citizen trust, reposition Eastern Europe (Ukraine, Moldova) as the resilience core rather than the periphery, and confront the EU's credibility gap from double standards and a stalled reform agenda.

themes.theme: Differentiated integration as the new EU operating model

insights.claim: Because a heterogeneous bloc of 27 (and eventually 30+) EU member states cannot move in lockstep, pro-European forces should treat differentiated (multi-speed) integration as a structural imperative and normal governance tool rather than an exceptional workaround.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Expect EU policy progress to keep concentrating in sub-groups of willing states (defense procurement, capital markets, fiscal tools) rather than uniform 27-country legislation; investors should track coalition-of-the-willing blocs as the real locus of near-term EU policy action.
insights.quote: In an age of permanent crisis, unity should not mean uniformity.
insights.locator: Differentiation in the Age of Permacrisis

insights.claim: A three-rung escalation ladder is proposed for advancing European integration when consensus fails: first, fuller use of existing treaty tools like enhanced cooperation and PESCO; second, an 'Open Supragovernmental Avant-Garde' (OSAG) of willing states operating alongside but outside the EU treaties (especially for defense); and third, as a last-resort contingency, a new supranational 'federal core' entity with binding majority decisionmaking and joint fiscal capacity.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: A federal 'core Europe' remains a tail scenario, but OSAG-style defense coalitions outside the treaties are the more probable near-term vehicle to watch for European defense-industrial and capability initiatives.

insights.claim: Since the 2008 financial crisis Europe has followed a repeating 'permacrisis' pattern in which each shock is defused but not structurally resolved: the 2009 eurozone crisis stabilized the currency without creating fiscal union, the 2015 migration crisis cut inflows without fixing governance, and the response to Russia's invasion of Ukraine has been unprecedented in scale but always capped by lowest-common-denominator politics.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Investors should expect the same pattern to repeat on current stress points (defense financing, competitiveness): crisis-driven half-measures that stabilize but don't structurally resolve, meaning recurring flashpoints rather than one-off fixes.
insights.locator: Differentiation in the Age of Permacrisis

insights.claim: A treaty-based framework for differentiated integration is proposed with three reforms: a formal procedure (modeled on enhanced cooperation) for subsets of states to deepen integration, symmetric safeguards preventing discrimination against new members who are kept in transitional exclusion, and new legal procedures for 'differentiated disintegration' letting states exit specific policy areas without leaving the EU entirely.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Watch for EU treaty-reform proposals that formalize opt-out/opt-in mechanisms; this would reduce the ad hoc bargaining that currently lets a handful of powerful states dictate the terms of flexibility.

insights.claim: The EU's reliance on ad hoc, informal differentiation has already forced integration outside the treaty framework: in 2012, British opposition to the EU's Fiscal Compact forced other member states to adopt it as a separate intergovernmental Treaty on Stability, Coordination, and Governance rather than as EU law.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: A single veto-holding member state can still push EU cooperation into parallel legal structures outside Brussels' institutional oversight, which is a recurring risk to legal coherence whenever unanimity is required.

insights.claim: A 'LEGO Europe' model for EU foreign policy is proposed in which the EU keeps consensus-based strategic direction-setting but organizes implementation into opt-in modular blocks (e.g., a defense-support module, an energy-resilience module, a reconstruction module for Ukraine, a diplomacy module for Iran) that can include non-EU partners like the UK, Norway, and Ukraine without requiring full accession or unanimous rollout.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: This decouples slow EU consensus politics from execution speed; sectors most likely to see modular fast-tracking are defense support, energy resilience, and reconstruction financing tied to Ukraine.

themes.theme: Eastern flank reframed as the resilience core, not the periphery

insights.claim: Ukraine and Moldova, long treated as part of a gray zone between Russia and the EU, should be repositioned as central pillars of EU 'societal resilience' architecture because their wartime and crisis-driven institutional adaptability outstrips that of stability-accustomed Western Europe.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Frontline states may become disproportionately influential in shaping future EU security and crisis-governance policy relative to their formal membership status, a signal for anyone tracking EU enlargement politics or Eastern European risk premia.
insights.locator: Rethinking EU Resilience: Bringing Europe's Edge to the Center

insights.claim: Moldova's most recent parliamentary elections produced a victory for the pro-European Party of Action and Solidarity (PAS) and the defeat of corrupt, pro-Russia, kleptocratic actors, evidencing an accelerating pro-EU political realignment in the immediate neighborhood.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: A durable pro-EU government in Moldova strengthens the case for accelerated accession and reduces near-term Russian-influence risk on the EU's eastern border.
insights.locator: Europe as Societal and Democratic Resilience

insights.claim: A 'reversible accession' principle is proposed under which EU membership becomes a tiered spectrum with clear benchmarks and timelines, applied symmetrically: candidate states like Ukraine get progressive institutional access as they meet benchmarks, while existing members that block collective decisions (Hungary under its previous government, potentially Slovakia) could be relegated to lower integration tiers.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: If adopted, this would create real downside consequences for obstructionist member states for the first time, changing the political calculus of veto-based blocking within the EU.

insights.claim: Public support for further EU enlargement is a real political constraint: the European Commission's September 2025 Eurobarometer survey found only 56 percent of EU citizens in favor of more EU enlargement, despite elite-level consensus around admitting Ukraine and Moldova.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Enlargement timelines could slip relative to elite rhetoric because of soft but real public resistance; watch national referenda or coalition politics as the binding constraint rather than Brussels-level agreement.
insights.quote: only 56 percent of EU citizens said they were in favor of more EU enlargement

insights.claim: Ukraine's civil society has continued to function as a check on government even amid full-scale war: the country's first wartime demonstration occurred in June 2025 after the government tried to strip powers from anticorruption bodies, and civil society pushed back successfully.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Active anticorruption civil-society pushback is a positive signal for Ukraine's institutional durability and governance quality, relevant to reconstruction-financing and accession-conditionality assessments.

themes.theme: The EU's agency crisis and the failure of technocratic reform

insights.claim: Expert-driven calls for radical EU reform (such as the Draghi and Letta reports) lack real force because they originate from a Brussels technocratic class disconnected from national democratic will-formation; genuine reform requires routing contested questions like European security architecture and AI policy back through national electoral politics rather than more expert commissions.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Top-down competitiveness/reform reports (Draghi-style) are unlikely to translate into binding action without a national-electorate mandate; treat such reports as agenda-setting signals, not policy catalysts.

insights.claim: Several headline post-2022 EU 'transformative' commitments have fallen short in practice: the SAFE defense financing facility is temporary rather than permanent; a year after Germany's March 2025 debt-brake reform freed hundreds of billions of euros for military and infrastructure spending, independent assessments found much of it had instead funded routine federal expenditure; and the roughly €100 billion ($116 billion) Franco-German joint fighter-jet program is likely to be scrapped or downgraded to a two-jet compromise.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Headline European defense-spending announcements should be discounted until execution data confirms funds are reaching intended capability programs rather than backfilling existing budgets.

insights.claim: To resolve the EU's 'agency crisis,' it is proposed that Europe hold direct national referenda on contested strategic questions, modeled on Spain's 1986 NATO membership referendum, asking citizens whether to remain in NATO, join a new European security organization, or forgo collective regional security altogether — even accepting the risk that different member states would choose different, mutually incompatible answers.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: A genuine pan-European referendum on NATO/strategic autonomy is a low-probability but high-impact scenario; if pursued, it could either cement or unwind decades of assumed transatlantic security consensus.

insights.claim: The EU's 2024 AI Act, rushed into law to shape global regulatory norms ahead of the US and China, was ill-suited to the large language models that emerged in late 2022 and failed to reflect the depth of concern across member states; the real European advantage over the US and China is not speed of regulation but the depth and quality of national debate about AI's ethical and social implications.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Contrarian read for AI-policy investors: bet against the narrative that EU AI regulation is simply slower/weaker than US-China; the author argues deliberative depth is itself Europe's differentiator, implying future EU AI rules may diverge substantively rather than just lag chronologically.

insights.claim: ECB President Christine Lagarde (formally in post until October 2027) and Bank of France Governor François Villeroy de Galhau are both expected to step down early, timed so that successors can be appointed before the 2027 French presidential election, in which a far-right candidate may win power.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Expect an ECB leadership transition to be finalized well before the 2027 French election; this is a concrete near-term catalyst for euro-area monetary-policy continuity risk to monitor.
insights.quote: Europe's political mainstream is doing as much as possible to prevent extremists from breaking into the liberal fortress.

insights.claim: Europe's far right has shifted strategy from seeking EU exit to reshaping the EU from within — French National Rally leader Jordan Bardella said in a May 2026 interview that he favors 'changing everything but without breaking anything' regarding Europe, exemplifying what one scholar calls 'Eurosceptic remainers' (alongside Austria's Freedom Party, Italy's Brothers of Italy, Slovakia's Smer, and Hungary's Fidesz), with the UK's 2020 exit now the outlier rather than the template.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Political risk from the European far right should be modeled as internal EU capture/reform-from-within risk rather than a wave of further EU exits — implications for how EU institutional continuity risk is priced going into 2027 French elections.
insights.quote: regarding Europe, I am in favor of changing everything but without breaking anything.

themes.theme: Manufacturing European identity and trust from the ground up

insights.claim: Effective EU institutions require prior social buy-in ('Europeanness') rather than better institutional design; drawing on Rousseau and Elinor Ostrom, the argument is that incremental, tangible wins — such as the elimination of mobile roaming charges and 1992 single-market mutual recognition of standards — build cumulative popular support more effectively than grand redesigns.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Policymakers seeking public buy-in for deeper EU integration should prioritize small, visible consumer/citizen-facing wins over sweeping treaty change, which this framework predicts will fail to build durable legitimacy.
insights.quote: Europe is not a design problem. Neither is governance, for that matter. These are people problems.

insights.claim: Four concrete proposals are floated to manufacture a practical European identity: a mandatory cross-border EU public-service requirement for 18-26 year-olds; EU-funded pan-European universities, technical schools, and shared research institutes (e.g., one AI-safety institute serving all of Europe instead of each large country building its own); an EU public-media entity modeled on U.S. NPR to counter disinformation and 'AI slop'; and adoption of English as a common second language taught alongside national languages in all public schools.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: These are far from current EU policy but flag where 'soft infrastructure' investment (EU-wide research institutes, shared AI-safety capacity) could reduce duplicated national R&D spend if political will emerges.

insights.claim: A 'Citizen Power Europe' vision proposes replacing the EU's 'ever-closer union' teleology with 'sustainable integration,' anchored by a permanent, itinerant Citizens' Assembly in which one-third of randomly selected members rotate out each year, designed to institutionalize deliberative democracy as a standing complement to elected representative bodies rather than a one-off consultation exercise.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: A standing EU-wide citizens' assembly, if implemented, would be a structural governance innovation worth tracking as a potential legitimacy mechanism distinct from European Parliament elections.

insights.claim: Make.org's 'EurHope' youth consultation, which gathered 1.5 million voices ahead of the 2024 European Parliament elections, found that young Europeans' top-ranked priority was more democratic EU institutions — ranking above economic or security concerns — suggesting legitimacy, not policy competence, is the binding generational constraint on deeper integration.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Youth-oriented EU political messaging and policy design should prioritize visible democratic accountability mechanisms over economic promises if this survey signal holds.

insights.claim: A 'European Democratic Digital Union' is proposed pairing continuous digital-participation platforms and cross-border translation/media tools with democratic reform, arguing that the reputation captured by the phrase 'America innovates, China replicates, and Europe regulates' reflects a reactive posture that a proactive digital strategy — leveraging Europe's linguistic and cultural diversity as an innovation asset rather than a barrier — could reverse.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Watch for EU digital-policy shifts that pair regulation with active innovation funding rather than regulation alone, as a signal the 'reactive Europe' narrative is being consciously countered.
insights.quote: America innovates, China replicates, and Europe regulates

insights.claim: The European Political Community (EPC), launched in 2022 and open to all European states (not just EU members), is proposed as the platform for a renewed pan-European 'community of trust' organized around four functional pillars — Peace (crisis mediation, potential mutual-assistance clause), Preparedness (civil protection, hybrid-threat and cybersecurity coordination), Public Goods (energy/digital/transport connectivity financed via the EIB and EBRD), and People (expanded Erasmus+, permanent transnational citizens' assemblies) — with an EU-provided secretariat for continuity.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
entities: European Bank for Reconstruction and Development
insights.so_what: The EPC is flagged as underutilized despite having 'lost some of its initial momentum'; a reinvigorated EPC could become a parallel venue for non-EU European states (UK, Balkans, Eastern Partnership states) to access EU-adjacent cooperation without full membership.

insights.claim: A three-stage staged-membership model is proposed for EU accession: first, an 'associated member' status once candidates meet governance/rule-of-law/foreign-policy-alignment benchmarks (no institutional vote, but a signal of irrevocable trajectory and increased funding); second, a transitional 'new member' status granting full prerogatives except veto rights; and third, automatic full membership after a defined compliance period.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: This staged model, echoed by German Chancellor Friedrich Merz's 'associated membership' idea, is the most concrete near-term accession compromise on the table for Ukraine; watch for convergence around a similar tiered proposal in actual EU enlargement negotiations.
insights.locator: A Revised Path to EU Membership

insights.claim: Commissioner for Defense and Space Andrius Kubilius's January 2026 proposal for a European Security Council, established via intergovernmental treaty and involving both EU and non-EU countries to fill capability gaps and handle crisis management while preserving NATO's operational system, is cited as the leading real-world application of the 'avant-garde' logic to defense policy.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: The Kubilius European Security Council proposal is a concrete policy vehicle to track for near-term European defense-institutional innovation outside strict EU treaty confines.

themes.theme: Europe's global credibility gap and double standards

insights.claim: The EU's foreign-policy credibility with the Global South is undermined by a documented pattern of double standards: it demanded global solidarity with Ukraine against Russia's invasion while remaining unwilling to similarly condemn Israel's military operations in Gaza and Lebanon, and gave only half-hearted, cacophonous responses on Venezuela, Greenland, and Iran.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Investors assessing EU soft-power leverage or Global South partnership initiatives (Global Gateway) should discount claimed EU normative credibility given this documented selectivity.
insights.locator: A Catalogue of Double Standards in EU Policies

insights.claim: The EU should abandon its 'geopolitical Europe' self-label and any ambition to become a classical or middle power, and instead lean into its actual comparative advantage: an asymmetric form of power built on trade, regulation, and normative/selective leverage, given that it remains structurally dependent on others (chiefly the US) for hard security.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Frames a realistic ceiling on EU 'strategic autonomy' rhetoric: expect continued EU dependence on external security guarantees even as its regulatory/trade leverage (AI Act, sanctions, Global Gateway) keeps expanding — two tracks moving at different speeds.

insights.claim: Rather than trying to preserve universal multilateralism as the US retreats, the EU should pursue a 'world minus one' strategy — narrow coalitions built around a small number of concrete global public goods (energy transition, maritime security, biodiversity, human-centered technology) where EU interests and shared global interest overlap, working with middle powers and existing institutions rather than waiting for universal consensus.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Expect EU external engagement to increasingly take the form of narrow, issue-specific coalitions (e.g., maritime security, critical minerals, climate) rather than broad multilateral pushes, especially as US engagement with multilateral institutions wanes.

themes.theme: Structural immobilism and the case for radical thinking

insights.claim: Despite repeated leader-level calls for fundamental EU redesign after the eurozone crisis, Brexit, the migration surge, COVID-19, and Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, the union's basic institutional settlement has remained essentially unchanged since the 2009 Lisbon Treaty, itself only a minor increment on arrangements settled in the early 2000s — evidence of deep structural path dependency (immobilism) despite an October 2025 letter from European leaders demanding the EU 'change its course, not just a little but substantially.'
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: Investors should heavily discount rhetoric-driven expectations of imminent EU institutional overhaul; the base case, per this 15+ year track record, is incremental adjustment even after major shocks.
insights.quote: we must change its course. Not just a little but substantially.

insights.claim: There is a growing structural mismatch between formal EU membership and actual policy-cooperation needs: non-EU European states share EU concerns on defending Ukraine and containing illiberalism, while EU integration remains vulnerable to being blocked by just one or two illiberal member governments playing spoiler.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: This mismatch is the structural rationale behind most of the compilation's proposals (EPC, OSAG, LEGO Europe) for pan-European cooperation frameworks that operate outside strict EU membership boundaries.

insights.claim: The editor proposes that European governments designate roughly 2028 as a moment of institutional 'refoundation,' preceded by an extended period of intensive citizen deliberation and participatory assemblies, culminating in a referendum offering citizens a genuinely wide range of options — from looser cooperation, to a multi-circle European order, to full state-like political union.
insights.speaker: Carnegie Endowment
insights.so_what: A 2028 EU 'refoundation referendum' is a speculative but explicitly proposed catalyst date worth flagging for long-horizon European political-risk scenario planning.
